We went over introductions, how it's best to have someone else do it for you and that it's best to do it the first thing, maybe even beforehand if you can through an email. We talked about how it's very powerful and that it can make us rethink our better judgment. He showed us some examples with experiments with the electric shock, which was quite shocking. We talked about triggers that someone could have that would make us think that they're an authority, like a uniform, a stethoscope, what kind of car they have. We talked about the difference between being in authority versus being in authority. We showed you that it was important whenever possible to show your authority by displaying your certificates and showing your certifications. And finally, we talked about how reviews by experts or testimonials about you can build your authority. So you wanna place them on your website and make sure that they are visible. That you've bought, you've tried. So she's not receptive. If someone's not receptive, then they're not going to be receptive. Yesterday during the class, one of the things I don't know about is, how do you introduce yourself on a Zoom call and establish your authority when there's nobody else to introduce you? Like all of us are on a call and you're the host and you just say, well, introduce yourself, but you are all contributing different perspectives to problem solving. How do you establish your authority in that situation where you're one of a group? Well, that would be one of those situations where you would wanna keep it as short and brief as possible. There's no way to do it beforehand in this situation and there's no way to have someone else do it for you. So with that said, the best thing to do is keep it as concise as possible because in that instance, you're forced to almost be bragging. So in that situation, keep it as little as possible. Okay, just the highlights about who you are. So is that where you use your title or... Yeah, use your title, maybe the top three things about you. Okay. Yeah. Any other questions? Alberto? This can really help, but you know, lots of people think it's just about influence and persuasion getting to a yes, but one of the things we really try to tell people is it's really a language to build relationships and a code of ethics to live by and to build those relationships and to make them really strong. And it's a constant thing. People say, well, I wanna do some reciprocity because I need something right now. Well, it's a big thing. You've gotta build that relationship and make it something substantial. So the, you know, it's not a reactive thing. It's not mechanical like that. There's a good story about how the power of words that Bob has, in the beginning, he called them weapons of influence in the beginning. And nobody really had any pushback until he spoke to a group of nuns at a convent. And they said, we really have, we put a lot of emphasis on the power of words. And that's just a real negative word and we don't use the word weapon in here. We don't have that in our vocabulary. And a lot of people would think, well, that's fine for them. But Bob really internalized it. And he realized the power of it and changed it to the principles of influence. And that's kind of some of the problems when we do the translations, when he's used words that are very American, bungler, smuggler, sleuth, we say. And so when he started things, he didn't really realize that it would be so big and international. And he didn't realize that he was gonna have to think of those things.
